rationale: AI-Scientist v2 uses literature search during ideation. This placeholder marks ideas as provisionally novel until a separate energy-domain novelty checker is added.

raw.Name: market_calibrated_flexibility_opf
raw.Title: Market-Calibrated Flexibility OPF: Learning Distribution-Level Demand and Storage Response Curves for Secure Transmission Operations
raw.Short Hypothesis: A key bottleneck in renewable-rich power systems is that transmission-level OPF and security-constrained dispatch treat distribution-connected flexibility from DERs, storage, and demand response either as fixed forecasts or as simplistic aggregate limits. The hypothesis is that system operators can achieve materially better reliability-cost tradeoffs by replacing these static representations with market-calibrated, context-dependent flexibility response curves that predict how much net injection a feeder or virtual power plant can actually deliver at different price and dispatch signals under network and device constraints. This is the right setting because the question is operational, economically grounded, and cannot be answered by generic forecasting alone: the object of interest is not load or generation itself, but realizable flexibility conditional on incentives and local constraints.
raw.Related Work: Prior work in power systems has studied AC/DC OPF, stochastic and robust OPF, transactive energy, virtual power plants, demand response aggregation, bilevel market models, and learning-assisted OPF. However, most strands make one of two simplifications: either flexibility is modeled with exogenous envelopes independent of incentives, or economic response is modeled without embedding physical feeder constraints and operational deliverability. Recent ML-for-OPF papers also often focus on accelerating solvers or forecasting uncertainty rather than learning physically realizable flexibility sets for coordination across transmission and distribution. This proposal distinguishes itself by introducing a compact response-curve object that maps system-level signals to feasible net flexibility at the feeder/VPP interface, calibrated from simulated or historical local dispatch outcomes and then embedded directly into security-constrained OPF. It is not a trivial extension of aggregate demand models because the learned object is explicitly constraint-aware, time-coupled through storage state, and evaluated by system-level security, cost, and deployability metrics.
raw.Abstract: High penetrations of distributed energy resources, flexible demand, and behind-the-meter storage create substantial latent flexibility, but today’s transmission-level optimization tools rarely exploit it well. A core reason is representation mismatch: distribution-level flexibility is often reduced to static upper/lower bounds, ignoring how actual response depends on incentive levels, local network congestion, device states, and temporal coupling. We propose Market-Calibrated Flexibility OPF (MCF-OPF), a framework that learns compact, context-dependent flexibility response curves for feeders or virtual power plants and embeds them into transmission operations. For each distribution area, a local optimizer or simulator generates feasible net power responses under varying price-like and dispatch-like signals, subject to AC feeder constraints, DER limits, storage dynamics, and comfort/service constraints. These responses are distilled into monotone, uncertainty-aware response curves conditioned on easily observable context such as time of day, weather, state of charge summaries, and feeder loading. The transmission operator then solves a security-constrained OPF or multi-period dispatch using these learned flexibility objects rather than static flexibility bands. The project tests whether this representation improves renewable integration, congestion management, reserve procurement, and post-contingency feasibility relative to conventional aggregate models. The approach is computationally modest, compatible with existing decomposition practices, and attractive for practical deployment because it exposes interpretable flexibility bids rather than opaque black-box controls. If successful, it would provide a new operational layer between distribution flexibility and bulk-system optimization, improving both economic efficiency and secure deliverability.
Experiments: Develop a benchmark with an IEEE transmission test case (e.g., RTS-GMLC or IEEE 118-bus) coupled to several IEEE distribution feeders hosting rooftop PV, batteries, EV charging, and thermostatic demand. Generate synthetic but realistic time series for weather, load, and DER availability.

Construct local feeder optimization problems that, for each time interval and context, compute feasible net power exchange under a grid of incentive/dispatch signals while enforcing AC power flow, voltage limits, thermal limits, inverter limits, storage SOC dynamics, and user comfort constraints. Use these solutions to fit piecewise-linear or monotone neural response curves with uncertainty bands.

Embed the learned response curves into day-ahead and real-time security-constrained DC-OPF or linearized AC-OPF. Compare against baselines: (1) no distribution flexibility, (2) static aggregate min/max flexibility bounds, (3) price-elastic but network-agnostic demand models, and (4) oracle coordination using full distribution optimization online.

Evaluate total operating cost, renewable curtailment, reserve procurement, frequency of transmission and distribution constraint violations, N-1 post-contingency infeasibility rate, flexibility delivery error, computational time, and out-of-sample robustness under forecast error.

Test sensitivity to context features and representation choices: scalar flexibility bounds vs piecewise-linear curves vs uncertainty-aware curves; inclusion/exclusion of storage state summaries; and feeder heterogeneity. Quantify when richer response curves justify their complexity.

Run market-oriented experiments where feeders submit flexibility bids derived from learned curves into a distribution-to-transmission coordination mechanism. Measure incentive compatibility proxies such as ex-post delivery shortfall and profit/regret of aggregators under realized uncertainty.

Perform ablations on training data source: historical operation traces versus optimization-generated labels versus hybrid datasets. Test transfer across seasons and DER penetration levels to assess practical data requirements.
Risk Factors and Limitations: If real or simulated local response is weakly dependent on incentives, gains over static flexibility envelopes may be modest.
Learned response curves may be miscalibrated under rare contingencies or regime shifts, requiring conservative uncertainty margins.
Coupling AC distribution constraints with transmission SCOPF may still require approximations; benefits could depend on linearization quality.
The proposal assumes sufficient observability of context variables such as aggregate SOC or feeder loading, which may not always be available.
Market realism is simplified unless detailed settlement and participant behavior models are included; early studies may therefore focus more on operational than strategic effects.
Results may depend on synthetic data and benchmark design, so demonstrating external validity to utility settings could require follow-on collaboration.
